sentences.text: Just like this, or smiling?
Good afternoon.
Helen-- maiden name Fromer-- Greenbaum.
Yes, Nechama.
After an aunt, a, a great aunt.
19-- Excuse me.
Warsaw, Poland.
Shlomo, Mattis, Shlomo.
Mattis Shlomo Fromer.
He dealt in soft leather business.
Well, he was selling leather for, to people that were making shoes, to making for umbrella handles, all kinds of things.
I, I, I really didn't participate in the knowledge of what business Dad was doing.
But a lot of people used to come up to the house also that he dealt with.
That I don't know.
I really don't know.
For some reason or other, my father did just about everything until-- just to make a nice, comfortable living for the family.
At the same address that we lived in.
Well, there was a kind of a basement where he kept the leathers.
And then they were picking it up from there.

Gensia, 55, which the, the spelling is different than the Polish spelling, because there the E after the G had a little, little [? thicky ?] [LAUGHS] under the E, which was-- but here they say G-E-N-S-I-A, Gensia.
My father-- first of all, I want to point out to you that people were running to work for my father.
Because he was the most generous person.
Because when came holidays, Saturdays, you know, for the Shabbat, they went-- they knew that there was going to be food on the table for their wives and children.
So that I am proud of, that my father was a generous person with his workers and keeping-- giving donations.
Couldn't ask for a better one.
The only thing is that-- I [LAUGHS] am sorry to stay, but I was a spoiled brat.
I can't say really spoiled brat, but I'm the youngest.
And, and there's a saying in-- back home, for the youngest child, [NON-ENGLISH], if you ever heard that expression.
And they were so proud [LAUGHS] even though that I was not expected anymore.
And I had a very-- for the 16 years until the war broke out, I had the most marvelous life, the most comfortable life.
Devora [?
Leia ?].
Franco ?].
Well, I'll tell you what-- my, my father, God rest his soul, he used to call her [NON-ENGLISH], which was pet name, like.
But whenever we heard him say Devora [NON-ENGLISH], that meant he's angry about something.
Otherwise, it was [?
Velishee ?].
My-- our mom was treated like a queen in the house.
And even though we had a live-in maid-- I don't remember ever being one day without a, without a live-in maid.
Yet, Momma always used to say, you shouldn't have to, but you should know how to, expecting that we will marry comfortable enough to, to have a maid again.
But my momma did the cooking and baking.
She was famous for it.
School was that we used to bring-- the people used to bring-- when there was parents' meetings, everybody brought something, and it was sold to each other.
And the money went for the poor children's milk or lunches.
I never went to school without one lunch-- always two because I sat in the same seat with a girl that was, had-- didn't have a father.
She was a half an orphan.
And my mom made sure that came holidays, got donated also different things for the holidays.
She had six, but we were five alive.
My oldest brother was Shimon.
My, the second one was [?
Leepa Mandel ?].
Third one was Eli, I would say-- Eli.
I'm trying to do the best I can [LAUGHS] with the Jewish names-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Then my sister, Raizel [?
Rolhome ?] Rosie, and myself, one name, Nechama.
I was the sixth one.
There was the fourth one was a girl that died when my sister was a year old.
I never knew her, because--
Scarlet fever.
Orthodox.
It described that-- in Warsaw, you had every few blocks, you had the synagogues.
So there was no such thing like you have to sometimes, you know, here that you don't live close enough to drive.
And mom used to make jokes about my dad, because he was always the first one to go to schul and the last one to come home.
And she always used to say, I don't mind if you open the schul, but why do you have to close it?
And one incident I remembered how he was respected that one year when I was very young that he was very sick during the high holidays.
And Rosh Hashanah, they came up to our house to blow shofar.
This stays with me.
[SOB] Sorry.
Was that a, a big honor?
This was the most-- the biggest honor you can do to a, a member to come to the house to blow shofar.
Because my father wasn't allowed to eat anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every Saturday, [LAUGHS] he came home from schul, there was never that he came by himself.
There was a-- you know what an oyrekh is?
So he came with an oyrekh, with a guest.
One person that liked to come very often with my father was a man that had the most famous restaurant, Jewish kosher restaurant in, in Warsaw.
Jeepers.
I'll, I'll think of it.
And he used to say that-- and he was famous for his meals.
And he said that nobody can compare with Mrs.
Fromer's cooking.
Well, I went to an all-girls school--
--which I don't know if, if it was on purpose or not.
But it was, it was just Jewish kids.
I encountered a lot of-- on the way to school, because it was also within walking distance-- a few blocks.
And I encountered a lot of racism from young kids.
Not just hearing.
They used to grab a child, a, a girl that had two braids and run over and cut one braid.
Yes.
And to me, I used to wear a, a, a, a coat.
I always forget the name of the-- I was always kind of a cold kid.
I never played with snow or any, anything.
And-- a lamb coat.
And one time, I was walking to school, and I feel a stab in my back.
And I thought, it's a knife.
And I grabbed whatever was there.
And it was a mouse-- dead mouse-- with a wire wrapped around the tail of the mouse and the other end stabbed into my coat.
And I never saw a mouse in my life before because we lived in a big city and never saw mice in the house.
And I developed a terrible, terrible [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] way of dealing with rodents.
But I, I encountered them eventually in the concentration camp where the bigger rodents were running over us-- the, the rats.
So that was my encounter with the racism from small children.
Your father-- did he-- was he able to provide well for your family?
Well, if a live-in maid was there-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I used to put my, my two cents in if she was good.
If she couldn't tell good stories, I used to tell Mom, she's, she's not going to be good.
Because I knew-- I wanted a maid that could tell stories, good stories.
Well, that's another thing.
We never had a non-Jewish maid.
Because in the small cities, in the shtetl, they used to have several children-- which is unheard of-- that they were very poor.
And they send in the girls to Warsaw to become maids.
Jewish families-- sorry to say.
That's why my mom always treated her part of the family also.
Because she didn't have to teach her Kashruts.
She knew what's what.
And never did we have anything but a Jewish girl working in our house.
Positively, positively.
The only time she wore a wig is when to the wedding, because my father's family demanded it.
But she--
To her own wedding-- because I understand, I found out later from my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] here in the United States, that-- most of the family was dead.
But she told me, did your mother ever tell you that she comes from the Jewish Orthodox, Jewish, Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of family that was the--
--the aristocrats?
I said no, Mom never talked about that.
That's why she said, she'll wear the sheitel, which wigs, to the wedding, but she won't wear one.
But she was a very religious person.
And came Shabbat, she was wearing a scarf.
She-- there was always Friday night dinners.
And after the dinner, we used to sit around and sing.
All kinds of songs, whatever we could think of.
I can't remember the song.
Sometimes we were singing-- because we used to go a lot to the Jewish theater.
So we also sang songs from the Jewish plays and some Hebrew songs.
Please-- you know something?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of dates also don't ring bells with me.
Because I like-- I talk with my sister today.
And I was talking about it.
And I said, for God's sakes, I must have blocked a lot of things out.
--that they used to come.
Mom had one-- Mom had one Jewish actress that was the most close friend.
Her name was Barska Fisher.
As a matter of fact, they, they had an, an ad in the paper that a granddaughter is looking for somebody if they knew a Barska Fisher from Warsaw.
Well, my sister called the newspaper.
And they gave the, the people the telephone number to call my sister.
Because this was an open house.
It's a matter of fact, my brother's friends, when it came Purim and Hanukkah, they showered my mom with gifts.
Because they didn't have such open home that they can always come find a meal, find somebody to talk to, spend some time.
And this was the-- and there, and there, there-- always happy, always songs, always so much, so much happiness in the house.
And so was with my girlfriends.
They used to come home with me from school and do homework with me.
Wipe my nose.
I have one here.
Sorry.
After all, I got misty.
And I, I don't need-- I think that maybe I need it.
I'm sorry.
All that happiness in the house-- always laughter.
And during the time when they closed up the ghetto, my mom used to cook.
At that time, we didn't have a maid.
The money was there, but the food was scarce, because the ration cards.
But whenever she cooked up a pot of soup, the next-door neighbor was getting a portion, and a flight down, the woman that just had a baby.
That's how we lived.
Mostly at home-- my mom-- they, they-- I had education at home, but yet--
We had a teacher to come to the house to teach.
I suppose so.
Because the-- even in the library, they said they are running out on books.
Because I was a bookworm.
No-- I'll tell you before [NON-ENGLISH]
When we were very young, we used to go to camps.
Summer camps-- I'm sorry, kosher camps, by the way.
Oh, God, I don't remember.
Most, most likely, because there were only Jewish kids in the camp.
Dear God, I don't remember.
I'm blocking out a lot of things.
Then when we got a little older, we were renting a bungalow in, in [NON-ENGLISH].
You could go by train.
You could go by car.
And I couldn't tell you how many miles-- kilometers.
Nothing-- just, just having a good time and having a lot of pictures taken-- that I have a whole album with pictures from [NON-ENGLISH]--
--but I didn't survive.
There were-- some of them were right here in the United States and some in Israel-- that I didn't survive any pictures.
I didn't come out from the concentration camp with pictures.
All those pictures I have, they were here-- my brother in the United States and my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] in the United States and also some family in Israel had pictures.
It was a beautiful place.
It was a whole, it was a whole line of resort places.
And, and we went every year there.
And we had, like, I have beautiful pictures taken.
And we just enjoyed.
Like my brother, that's the youngest from the boys, he used to rent a cart with a horse and put us all in the cart and take us for rides.
He just knew everything how to do.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he took a lot of pictures also, because he was-- he liked to dibble, to dabble into the photography also.
There were several Jewish theaters.
We were-- the whole house.
Yes, you went either Saturday night if it was, you know, Shabbos was out early, or you went Sunday.
Because it was playing all week.
That's why I don't enjoy things.
I saw [NON-ENGLISH], which here, it is-- it's in English, and it's dancing.
And it's--
Maybe I, I was in me that I saw it as a drama.
And to me, all that dancing-- and, and I just-- it doesn't register that, that good with me.
[INAUDIBLE] I don't remember this way.
And I remember.
Because I was a very young girl that I started to go to the theater.
It's a matter of fact, I met some actors here in the United States that I was very friendly with--
Bozyk.
Reizl Bozyk.
And they-- we were very friendly.
Reizl Bozyk used to hold me on, on her lap and play with me.
Because we used to go behind the--
Yeah-- to say hello.
And Raizel Bozek and her husband, Max Bozek, they remembered us, my sister and myself.
Well, I only had school until 16-- three, when the war-- when-- then June, when I would turn 16, there was vacation time.
And then the-- when the Nazis walked in-- you understand, I don't say Germans.
Because I, I hate when somebody blames a whole people.
So I always say the Nazis.
They're Hitler's henchmen.
When they walked in, the first thing is school out for children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had to turn in furs.
Even if it was a fur collar, you had to turn that in.
We also had to turn in radios.
But being that radios were usually registered, because you paid, you know, monthly-- but we had a radio that was disconnected.
Because my niece died, and we disconnected the, the radio.
So they didn't have any, any, anything to show that we have a radio.
So we could listen to the radio for quite a while.
When the war begin, it was a shock.
Because I, I heard stories about the First World War, but, you know, you can't imagine something when you don't witness it.
And when they started to bombard, and people were killed-- neighbors were killed.
People were killed.
And I just-- it was impossible to, to, think of something as horrible as something like that, that people can come and kill people for nothing.
In our home.
During the, during the day when they were bombing, we were trying to, to hide in cellars and, and places.
It's a matter of fact, I'm sorry to say, but in our street, there was a, a drug store.
It's not a drug store-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- because it was just, you know--
Yeah, because it was only for medications and things.
And we were very friendly with those people.
And they used to hide us during the day.
No-- not Jewish people.
That's the, that's the idea.
We were--
--hiding during the day in the very deep basement.

And a lot of people came to say, hey, you hiding Jews, and us, you don't let us in-- which-- that's when I started to-- I encountered-- I'll tell you about my my, my father that encountered something.
Well, anyhow, that was an experience also.
So one time when we were hiding, my father figured that-- so we bought a, a padlock on the door.
And he said, lock me in.
They'll see a padlock.
Because when they started to come to pick up people into the trucks to take to the Umschlagplatz, my father said, they'll see a padlock.
They not going to figure that nobody's home.
And my mom, my sister, my brother, her husband-- and we were hiding.
And when we came up after the-- because we had a super, which was not Jewish, but the nicest guy.
Because you see, you cannot judge a whole people of some bad, of some bad things.
And he used to let us know when it's safe to come out from the hiding place.
And when we came up, the, the Nazis took the door out from the hinges.
He was by myself himself in the house.
He just didn't feel like sitting in the basement with us.
So that's when we lost our father.
And don't ask me for the date.
I don't remember the dates.
I told you.
And he said, you have to stay in the house.
Because they used to pick up people were dying in the streets.
And then-- and they, and they were picking them up from the street.
First they covered up with any paper they could get hold of and then were picking them up with a, with a cart to the cemetery.
And that's when they started with the, with digging big, big, big holes and make a, a, a--
Just throw everybody in.
Nothing.
My-- at that time, I took over.
There, there was no maid, so I tried cleaning, doing things.
But you see, there's always-- it's a funny thing.
I don't know-- I can only say-- and the worst thing, we had a neighbor.
Can I finish the sentence?
The neighbor, she said-- she was a widow for long years.
She said, oh, good, I'll wind up in the grave with, with man in it, as a joke.
Because they had already-- they were putting children, men, women in, in the big ditch.
Thank you.
